tasks.title: Warm-up — Observe Partition Assignment With and Without Keys Using the Console
tasks.prompt: Create a topic called 'events' with 4 partitions, then use the console producer to send (1) 5 messages without a key and (2) 3 messages each with keys 'user1' and 'user2'. After sending, read each partition individually and record how the messages were distributed.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create the topic
kafka-topics.sh --create --topic events \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions 4 --replication-factor 1

# 2. Verify topic details
kafka-topics.sh --describe --topic events \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

# 3. Send 5 messages without a key
# (run the command below, type msg1 through msg5 one per line, then Ctrl+C)
kafka-console-producer.sh --topic events \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

# 4. Send messages with keys
# (format: key:value — e.g., user1:login, user1:view, user1:logout,
#  user2:login, user2:purchase, user2:logout)
kafka-console-producer.sh --topic events \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --property parse.key=true \
  --property key.separator=:

# 5. Check messages per partition (repeat for partitions 0 through 3)
kafka-console-consumer.sh --topic events \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partition 0 --from-beginning
# Repeat for partitions 1, 2, and 3 in the same way
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create the topic
kafka-topics.sh --create --topic events \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions 4 --replication-factor 1

# 2. Send 5 messages without a key (using a pipe)
for msg in msg1 msg2 msg3 msg4 msg5; do
  echo "$msg" | kafka-console-producer.sh --topic events \
    --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 2>/dev/null
done
# Result: the sticky partitioner batches messages to one partition at a time.
# With only 5 messages and linger.ms=0, most may land on the same partition.

# 3. Send 6 messages with keys (3 for user1, 3 for user2)
for msg in login view logout; do
  echo "user1:$msg" | kafka-console-producer.sh --topic events \
    --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
    --property parse.key=true \
    --property key.separator=: 2>/dev/null
done
for msg in login purchase logout; do
  echo "user2:$msg" | kafka-console-producer.sh --topic events \
    --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
    --property parse.key=true \
    --property key.separator=: 2>/dev/null
done

# 4. Check messages per partition (with --max-messages for quick verification)
for p in 0 1 2 3; do
  echo "=== Partition $p ==="
  kafka-console-consumer.sh --topic events \
    --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
    --partition $p --from-beginning \
    --max-messages 20 --timeout-ms 3000 \
    --property print.key=true 2>/dev/null
done

# Expected result (based on murmur2 hash with 4 partitions):
#   user1 -> hash("user1") % 4 = a specific partition (e.g., partition 1)
#   user2 -> hash("user2") % 4 = a different partition (e.g., partition 3)
# Confirm that user1's login/view/logout appear on the same partition in order,
# and user2's messages appear together on a different partition in order.
tasks.explanation: Kafka's default partitioner uses the murmur2 hash function to compute hash(key) % partitionCount and route each message accordingly. The same string key always produces the same hash, so it always maps to the same partition. This is the mechanism behind Kafka's 'intra-partition ordering guarantee' for a given key. Without a key, the sticky partitioner fills up a batch on one partition before moving to the next. Reading each partition directly from the console makes this contrast immediately visible.
hints: Add --property print.key=true to the consumer command to display both the key and the value, making it easy to see which key landed on which partition.
If opening an interactive console producer for every message is too tedious, use echo "key:value" | kafka-console-producer.sh ... to pipe messages in one at a time.

If the partition numbers don't match your expectation, run kafka-run-class.sh kafka.tools.GetOffsetShell --topic events --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 to check the per-partition offset and confirm where messages actually landed.

tasks.title: Core Lab 1 — Verify Key-Based Ordering with a Java Producer
tasks.prompt: Use a Java KafkaProducer to send events for three users (user1 through user3) in sequence, and verify via the partition() field of RecordMetadata that all messages for each user land on the same partition. The events for user1 should be sent in the order [login, view, purchase, logout], interleaved with events from the other two users.
tasks.explanation: producer.send(record).get() performs a synchronous send and returns RecordMetadata immediately. Recording the returned meta.partition() in a Map and throwing an exception if the same key is assigned a different partition provides a live ordering-guarantee validator. Running the program shows that user1's four messages (login → view → purchase → logout) always land on the same partition number, regardless of the interleaved messages from other users. This is Kafka's actual mechanism for guaranteeing the ordering of events for a specific key. Switching to async sends (callbacks) raises throughput but can mix up the print order, so synchronous sends are used here for clear verification.
hints: producer.send(record) returns a Future<RecordMetadata>. Appending .get() blocks until the broker responds and extracts the RecordMetadata.

Map.merge(key, value, remappingFunction) inserts the value if the key is absent, or applies the remappingFunction to the existing and new values otherwise. Here, the function throws an exception if the old and new partition numbers differ — acting as a validation guard.

To pre-calculate which partition a key will map to, compute Utils.murmur2(key.getBytes()) & 0x7FFFFFFF and take the result modulo partitionCount. This matches the formula used by Kafka's internal DefaultPartitioner — compare the result with the actual output to confirm they match.

tasks.title: Core Lab 2 — Reproduce a Hot Partition and Resolve Skew with a Composite Key
tasks.prompt: Reproduce a hot partition scenario in code by concentrating traffic on a single key, then resolve the skew by splitting into a composite key (userId + bucket number). Specifically: (1) send 1000 messages using the single key 'vip_user' to create a hot partition where all messages land on one partition, then (2) re-send 1000 messages using keys 'vip_user-0' through 'vip_user-3' (4 buckets) and compare the per-partition message counts.
tasks.explanation: Sending 1000 messages with the single key 'vip_user' means hash('vip_user') % 4 always produces the same result, so all 1000 messages pile up on one partition. The broker and consumer handling that partition get overloaded while the other three sit completely idle — the benefit of having four partitions is entirely wasted. Switching to composite keys 'vip_user-0' through 'vip_user-3' produces four distinct keys that hash to different partitions, distributing traffic evenly. In production, the bucket suffix would typically be based on a user attribute (region, signup date range, random suffix, etc.) rather than a simple modulo. Keep in mind the trade-off: composite keys guarantee ordering only within each sub-key, not across the entire 'vip_user' space.
hints: producer.flush() pushes all buffered messages to the broker immediately. Calling f.get() inside the send loop without flushing first can cause blocking before the batch is ready, making performance very slow. The correct pattern is: send all messages first, then flush(), then collect results with get().

If counts.size() == 1 in the single-key result, the hot partition is successfully reproduced. If counts.size() >= SPLITS and the distribution is roughly even in the composite-key result, the skew has been resolved.
Try varying the splits value — 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 — and re-running to visually observe how the distribution becomes progressively more even as the number of buckets increases.

tasks.title: Advanced — Implement a Priority-Routing Custom Partitioner Using the Partitioner Interface
tasks.prompt:
Implement a custom partitioner that satisfies the following business rules and register it with the producer.

Rules:
1. If the key starts with 'VIP:', route to partition 0 (dedicated fast-lane).
2. If the key starts with 'BATCH:', route to partition (numPartitions - 1) (background processing lane).
3. All other keys are hash-distributed evenly across partitions 1 through numPartitions-2.

Also add a guard that throws an IllegalStateException if numPartitions is less than 3.
tasks.explanation: The key insight of a custom partitioner is that the integer returned by partition() is the destination partition number. Three branches enforce the business rules at the infrastructure level: VIP isolation, BATCH demotion to a background lane, and even hash distribution for general traffic. Math.floorMod(keyStr.hashCode(), availablePartitions) is used instead of the % operator because Java's String.hashCode() can return negative values — % with a negative operand returns a negative result, while Math.floorMod always returns a non-negative value in [0, availablePartitions). The numPartitions < 3 guard prevents a subtle bug where numPartitions - 2 equals 0, causing the general-key partition to overlap with VIP partition 0. Registering via props.put('partitioner.class', ...) applies this partitioner to all topics for that producer instance; to restrict it to specific topics, add a conditional branch on the topic parameter inside partition().
hints: Math.floorMod(a, b) always returns a value between 0 and b-1, even when a is negative. Java's % operator returns a negative result for negative operands, so always use Math.floorMod or the pattern (hashCode & 0x7FFFFFFF) % N when computing partition numbers.

The value of props.put('partitioner.class', 'PriorityPartitioner') must be the fully qualified class name (FQCN). If the class is in the package com.example, use 'com.example.PriorityPartitioner'. A wrong name will cause a ClassNotFoundException at startup.

You can unit-test the partitioner logic without a running Kafka cluster. Either mock the Cluster object, or extract the core branching logic into a standalone method that takes (keyStr, numPartitions) as arguments and test it as plain Java.
